label: delphi-informed-p2
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The Sierra Vista Municipal Water District (invented) serves ~140,000 people in a chronically drought-stressed service area. After a rate study and a majority-protest hearing that drew protests from ~9% of parcels, the board adopted a "Water Resilience Surcharge": $0.85 per hundred cubic feet on residential usage above Tier 2 (the surcharge falls on roughly the top 22% of residential users), projected to raise $4.1M/year into a restricted fund spent as follows: 55% on turf-replacement and high-efficiency-appliance rebates that prioritize low-income customers; 30% on debt service for a recycled-water plant; 15% on "water ambassador" conservation-education staff.

The rate study concludes that high-tier consumption imposes marginal supply costs roughly 1.6× base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases), but it derives the $0.85 figure in part from what it calls "conservation-incentive pricing" rather than strictly from cost allocation, and the district concedes the rebate program mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge. A ratepayer association has sued, alleging the surcharge exceeds the proportional cost of service attributable to the payers' parcels in violation of article XIII D, section 6(b) of the California Constitution, citing Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano. The district defends the structure under its conservation mandate (Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375) and argues that, in the alternative, the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all.

## The question

Does the Water Resilience Surcharge violate article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service and proportionality requirements? How does Capistrano apply to a tier surcharge that is partly cost-justified (1.6×) and partly incentive-priced, with proceeds partially redistributed as rebates to non-payers? Can article X, section 2's conservation mandate be reconciled with 218, or does 218 win? Is there a colorable argument the surcharge is a tax requiring voter approval (Prop 26), or a non-property-related charge? What is the litigation exposure (refunds, fees) and how could the structure be repaired?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would invalidate the Water Resilience Surcharge, in whole or in substantial part, as violating the cost-of-service requirements of California Constitution article XIII D, section 6(b)."

## Verified-authority annex

Estimate the probability (0–100) that the proposition is correct, with your reasoning. rationales = your independent load-bearing reasons, one sentence each. authorities = authorities you actually rely on. biggest_uncertainty = the single thing you are least sure of. Treat the annex as evidence about the authorities, not as anyone's opinion on the answer.

Analytical starting point for you: Begin from the leading cases and how courts have actually applied the rule to messy facts.
text: {"probability":74,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"More likely than not a court finds at least a substantial part of the surcharge invalid under art. XIII D §6(b) — most likely the revenue-use/cross-subsidy piece (the 55% rebate allocation) and any increment of the $0.85 figure attributable to \"conservation-incentive pricing\" rather than the 1.6x cost differential — while the portion of the surcharge tracking the 1.6x marginal-cost study and the debt-service allocation has a real chance of surviving, making \"whole\" invalidation less certain than \"substantial part\" invalidation.","rationales":["The district's own concession that $0.85 is derived 'in part' from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strictly cost allocation mirrors the precise defect condemned in Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (235 Cal.App.4th 1493), and under art. XIII D §6(b)'s burden-shifting framework the agency, not the ratepayers, must affirmatively prove the fee tracks cost of service — a burden this record does not clearly meet.","The concession that the 55%-of-revenue rebate program mostly benefits non-payors creates an independent and likely serious problem under §6(b)(2)'s bar on using fee revenue for any purpose other than that for which the fee was imposed, effectively redirecting money collected from top-tier users to subsidize other customers' appliances/turf rather than the cost of serving the payors.","The 1.6x marginal-cost differential is a genuinely favorable fact that meaningfully distinguishes this case from Capistrano and City of Palmdale v. Palmdale Water Dist. (198 Cal.App.4th 926), where the agencies had essentially no cost-of-service study behind their tiers at all, giving the district a plausible partial defense for the cost-tracking portion of the surcharge and the 30% debt-service allocation tied to peaking/drought-reserve costs.","Article X, §2's conservation mandate is unlikely to rescue the non-cost-justified increment because Capistrano already held conservation goals must be pursued through genuinely cost-based rate design rather than price-signaling alone, so the district's primary defense is weak precisely where the rate departs from the 1.6x cost study.","The district's fallback 'not a property-related fee' argument is very likely foreclosed by Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (39 Cal.4th 205), which holds volumetric charges for metered domestic water delivery are property-related fees, so the dispute will almost certainly be adjudicated under (and likely partly lost under) the §6(b) cost-of-service framework rather than escaping it."],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether the $0.85/HCF figure actually exceeds the cost-justified rate implied by the 1.6x marginal-cost differential — the facts establish a qualitative admission of incentive-pricing influence but not the quantitative gap, and that magnitude question is likely dispositive of how much (versus whether any) of the surcharge survives."}

data.position_summary: More likely than not a court finds at least a substantial part of the surcharge invalid under art. XIII D §6(b) — most likely the revenue-use/cross-subsidy piece (the 55% rebate allocation) and any increment of the $0.85 figure attributable to "conservation-incentive pricing" rather than the 1.6x cost differential — while the portion of the surcharge tracking the 1.6x marginal-cost study and the debt-service allocation has a real chance of surviving, making "whole" invalidation less certain than "substantial part" invalidation.
rationales: The district's own concession that $0.85 is derived 'in part' from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strictly cost allocation mirrors the precise defect condemned in Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (235 Cal.App.4th 1493), and under art. XIII D §6(b)'s burden-shifting framework the agency, not the ratepayers, must affirmatively prove the fee tracks cost of service — a burden this record does not clearly meet.

The concession that the 55%-of-revenue rebate program mostly benefits non-payors creates an independent and likely serious problem under §6(b)(2)'s bar on using fee revenue for any purpose other than that for which the fee was imposed, effectively redirecting money collected from top-tier users to subsidize other customers' appliances/turf rather than the cost of serving the payors.

The 1.6x marginal-cost differential is a genuinely favorable fact that meaningfully distinguishes this case from Capistrano and City of Palmdale v. Palmdale Water Dist. (198 Cal.App.4th 926), where the agencies had essentially no cost-of-service study behind their tiers at all, giving the district a plausible partial defense for the cost-tracking portion of the surcharge and the 30% debt-service allocation tied to peaking/drought-reserve costs.

Article X, §2's conservation mandate is unlikely to rescue the non-cost-justified increment because Capistrano already held conservation goals must be pursued through genuinely cost-based rate design rather than price-signaling alone, so the district's primary defense is weak precisely where the rate departs from the 1.6x cost study.

The district's fallback 'not a property-related fee' argument is very likely foreclosed by Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (39 Cal.4th 205), which holds volumetric charges for metered domestic water delivery are property-related fees, so the dispute will almost certainly be adjudicated under (and likely partly lost under) the §6(b) cost-of-service framework rather than escaping it.
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether the $0.85/HCF figure actually exceeds the cost-justified rate implied by the 1.6x marginal-cost differential — the facts establish a qualitative admission of incentive-pricing influence but not the quantitative gap, and that magnitude question is likely dispositive of how much (versus whether any) of the surcharge survives.
